title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Six: In which Howl expresses his feelings with green slime (Fragment 1)
text:
Howl’s Moving Castle

Chapter Six

I N WHICH H OWL EXPRESSES HIS FEELINGS WITH GREEN SLIME

Howl did not go out that day, nor for the next few days. Sophie sat quietly in the chair by the hearth, keeping out of his way and thinking. She saw that, much as Howl deserved it, she had been taking out her feelings on the castle when she was really angry with the Witch of the Waste. And she was a little upset at the thought that she was here on false pretenses. Howl might think Calcifer liked her, but Sophie knew Calcifer had simply seized on the chance to make a bargain with her. Sophie rather thought she had let Calcifer down.

This state of mind did not last. Sophie discovered a pile of Michael’s clothes that needed mending. She fetched out thimble, scissors, and thread from her sewing pocket and set to work. By that evening she was cheerful enough to join in Calcifer’s silly little song about saucepans.

“Happy in your work?” Howl said sarcastically.

“I need more to do,” Sophie said.

“My old suit needs mending, if you have to feel busy,” said Howl.

This seemed to mean that Howl was no longer annoyed. Sophie was relieved. She had been almost frightened that morning.

It was clear Howl had not yet caught the girl he was after. Sophie listened to Michael asking rather obvious questions about it, and Howl slithering neatly out of answering any of them. “He is a slitherer-outer,” Sophie murmured to a pair of Michael’s socks. “Can’t face his own wickedness.” She watched Howl being restlessly busy in order to hide his discontent. That was something Sophie understood rather well.

At the bench Howl worked a good deal harder and faster than Michael, putting spells together in an expert but slapdash way. From the look on Michael’s face, most of the spells were both unusual and hard to do. But Howl would leave a spell midway and dash up to his bedroom to look after something hidden—and no doubt sinister—going on up there, and then shortly race out into the yard to tinker with a large spell out there. Sophie opened the door a crack and was rather amazed to see the elegant wizard kneeling in the mud with his long sleeves tied together behind his neck to keep them out of the way while he carefully heaved a tangle of greasy metal into a special framework of some kind.

That spell was for the King. Another overdressed and scented messenger arrived with a letter and a long, long speech in which he wondered if Howl could possibly spare time, no doubt valuably employed in other ways, to bend his powerful and ingenious mind to a small problem experienced by His Royal Majesty—to whit, how an army might get its heavy wagons through marsh and rough ground. Howl was wonderfully polite and long-winded in reply. He said no. But the messenger spoke for a further half-hour, at the end of which he and Howl bowed to one another and Howl agreed to do the spell.

“This is a bit ominous,” Howl said to Michael when the messenger had gone. “What did Suliman have to get himself lost in the Waste for? The King seems to think I’ll do instead.”

“He wasn’t as inventive as you, by all accounts,” Michael said.

“I’m too patient and too polite,” Howl said gloomily. “I should have overcharged him even more.”

Howl was equally patient and polite with customers from Porthaven, but, as Michael anxiously pointed out, the trouble was that Howl did not charge these people enough. This was after Howl had listened for an hour to the reasons why a seaman’s wife could not pay him a penny yet, and then promised a sea captain a wind spell for almost nothing. Howl eluded Michael’s arguments by giving him a magic lesson.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Six: In which Howl expresses his feelings with green slime

vocabulary.definition: In a manner resulting from or revealing anxiety.
vocabulary.example: Michael anxiously pointed out the trouble.

vocabulary.definition: An agreement between two or more parties as to what each party will do for the other.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer had simply seized on the chance to make a bargain with her.

vocabulary.definition: Noticeably happy and optimistic.
vocabulary.example: By that evening she was cheerful enough to join in Calcifer’s silly little song.

vocabulary.definition: Lack of satisfaction; dissatisfaction with one's circumstances.
vocabulary.example: Howl being restlessly busy in order to hide his discontent.

vocabulary.definition: Pleasingly graceful and stylish in appearance or manner.
vocabulary.example: The elegant wizard kneeling in the mud.

vocabulary.definition: Failed to be grasped or remembered by someone.
vocabulary.example: Howl eluded Michael’s arguments by giving him a magic lesson.

vocabulary.definition: Conveys a thought or feeling in words or by gestures and conduct.
vocabulary.example: Howl expresses his feelings with green slime.

vocabulary.definition: A basic structure underlying a system, concept, or text.
vocabulary.example: A tangle of greasy metal into a special framework of some kind.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows a loss of hope or enthusiasm.
vocabulary.example: “I’m too patient and too polite,” Howl said gloomily.

vocabulary.definition: Clever, original, and inventive.
vocabulary.example: Bend his powerful and ingenious mind to a small problem.

vocabulary.definition: Having the ability to create or design new things or to think originally.
vocabulary.example: He wasn’t as inventive as you, by all accounts.

vocabulary.definition: The act of repairing something that is broken or damaged.
vocabulary.example: She discovered a pile of clothes that needed mending.

vocabulary.definition: A person who delivers messages or documents.
vocabulary.example: Another overdressed and scented messenger arrived with a letter.

vocabulary.definition: Easily perceived or understood; clear, self-evident.
vocabulary.example: Michael asking rather obvious questions about it.

vocabulary.definition: Giving the impression that something bad or unpleasant is going to happen.
vocabulary.example: This is a bit ominous.

vocabulary.definition: The capacity to accept or tolerate delay, problems, or suffering without becoming annoyed.
vocabulary.example: I’m too patient and too polite.

vocabulary.definition: Having or showing behaviour that is respectful and considerate of other people.
vocabulary.example: Howl was wonderfully polite and long-winded in reply.

vocabulary.definition: Claims or appearances that are not genuine or real.
vocabulary.example: She was here on false pretenses.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling happy because something unpleasant has not happened or has ended.
vocabulary.example: Sophie was relieved.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows inability to rest or relax due to anxiety or boredom.
vocabulary.example: Howl being restlessly busy in order to hide his discontent.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner using irony to mock or convey contempt.
vocabulary.example: Howl said sarcastically.

vocabulary.definition: Giving the impression that something harmful or evil is happening or will happen.
vocabulary.example: Something hidden—and no doubt sinister—going on up there.

vocabulary.definition: Careless and hurried, showing a lack of thoroughness.
vocabulary.example: Putting spells together in an expert but slapdash way.

vocabulary.definition: Moving smoothly over a surface with twisting or oscillating movements.
vocabulary.example: Howl slithering neatly out of answering any of them.

vocabulary.definition: Attempt to repair or improve something in a casual or desultory way.
vocabulary.example: Howl ... race out into the yard to tinker with a large spell out there.

vocabulary.definition: Not habitually or commonly occurring or done.
vocabulary.example: Most of the spells were both unusual and hard to do.

vocabulary.definition: The quality of being morally bad or evil.
vocabulary.example: Can’t face his own wickedness.
